refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, visibility, and operator/system decision-making. This supports both autonomous optimization and human-in-the-loop control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_ton_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 1 week of representative operations). Compare against E_target. Verify that the NGHE solution provides real-time telemetry, control inputs, and feedback to enable this measurement and optimization.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved) with sufficient granularity and accuracy to compute energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time feedback to operators and tele-operators on current energy efficiency status and deviations from target.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall support control strategies (manual, semi-autonomous, or autonomous) that enable operators to optimize energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and archive energy and work data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that monitors vehicle power draw, work output, and operator commands; compute energy_per_ton in real-time; and provide feedback via telemetry to operators and tele-operators.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across vehicle subsystems (powertrain, hydraulics, auxiliary) with local optimization logic; aggregate data via telemetry for global energy_per_ton computation and feedback.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle-level energy sensors and local efficiency optimization (DP-B foundation) integrated with a centralized energy management service that computes KPI, detects anomalies, and provides operator feedback via telemetry and SafetyAlert channels.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid energy management architecture combining vehicle-level distributed monitoring and local optimization with centralized KPI computation, anomaly detection, and multi-channel feedback (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) to operators and tele-operators.

potential_sources: Energy measurement accuracy depends on sensor calibration and vehicle subsystem integration (Vehicle module dependency).
Real-time feedback latency depends on Telemetry interface bandwidth and SafetyAlert routing (Network Link dependency).
Operator control strategies must be compatible with vehicle actuation limits and safety constraints (Vehicle and Safety Services coupling).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits acceptable coupling: it decouples local vehicle optimization (owned by Vehicle) from global KPI computation (owned by NGHE Solution or Safety Services), reducing monolithic dependencies. Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces are well-defined and reusable across multiple FRs.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle subsystems expose energy consumption data via standardized interfaces (e.g., CAN bus, OBD-II equivalent). Risk: Legacy or proprietary vehicle architectures may require custom adapters.

Assumption: Network Link (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) provides sufficient bandwidth and latency for real-time feedback. Risk: Bandwidth constraints or network outages may delay feedback, reducing operator responsiveness.
Assumption: Operators and tele-operators can interpret energy efficiency feedback and adjust behavior accordingly. Risk: Lack of training or unclear feedback presentation may limit effectiveness.
Assumption: E_target is well-defined and achievable with the selected vehicle and operational profile. Risk: Unrealistic targets may lead to unsafe or unproductive control strategies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between vehicle-level optimization (e.g., gear selection, engine RPM tuning) and centralized KPI feedback. Specify the data schema for energy consumption telemetry (e.g., power in kW, work output in tons, timestamp). Define SafetyAlert thresholds (e.g., alert if energy_per_ton exceeds E_target by >10%).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy management service responsible for aggregating energy and work data from Vehicle subsystems, computing energy_per_ton KPI, detecting anomalies, and orchestrating feedback to operators and tele-operators via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. Includes data logging and archival for post-evaluation analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: Receive energy consumption (kWh), work output (tons), and vehicle state data from Vehicle subsystems. Outbound: Send computed energy_per_ton, efficiency status, and recommendations to operators and tele-operators.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound: Send energy efficiency anomalies or threshold violations (e.g., energy_per_ton exceeds E_target) to Safety Services and operators for immediate awareness and corrective action.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: Receive work orders and operational parameters (e.g., target energy_per_ton, evaluation period) from Site Manager or tele-operator to configure KPI computation and feedback thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying transport for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking; ensures reliable and timely data exchange between Vehicle, NGHE Solution, and operators.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of centralized KPI computation, anomaly detection, and multi-channel feedback orchestration. Energy management logic is cohesive and isolated from vehicle-specific subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to Vehicle (via Telemetry) and operators (via Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking).

Moderate containment: Vehicle owns energy sensors and local optimization logic (e.g., powertrain efficiency tuning). However, global KPI computation and feedback orchestration are not vehicle-specific and would be duplicated if assigned here. High diffusion: energy management logic would scatter across multiple vehicle subsystems (powertrain, hydraulics, auxiliary).

Moderate containment: Safety Services could own SafetyAlert generation for energy efficiency anomalies. However, KPI computation and feedback orchestration are not safety-specific. High diffusion: energy management logic would be entangled with safety-critical logic, complicating testing and certification.

Low containment: Teleoperation is a user interface and control channel, not a logical owner of energy management. Assigning here would couple energy management to tele-operator-specific workflows. High diffusion: energy management logic would be duplicated across tele-operator and on-site operator interfaces.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal owner because it centralizes energy management logic (KPI computation, anomaly detection, feedback orchestration) at the system level, avoiding duplication across vehicle subsystems or operator interfaces. The module boundary is clean: Vehicle provides energy data via Telemetry; NGHE Solution processes and feeds back via Telemetry and SafetyAlert; operators consume feedback via Telemetry and SafetyAlert.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic hub if additional system-level services (e.g., productivity optimization, maintenance prediction) are assigned to it. Mitigation: Define clear service boundaries and use interface-based composition.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become overloaded if too many data streams (energy, work output, vehicle state, diagnostics) are multiplexed. Mitigation: Prioritize data streams and use separate Telemetry channels for different data types.

Risk: SafetyAlert may be misused for non-safety-critical energy anomalies, diluting its safety significance. Mitigation: Define clear thresholds for SafetyAlert (e.g., only for energy_per_ton > E_target + 20%) and use Telemetry for routine feedback.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the data schema for Telemetry (energy consumption, work output, timestamps, vehicle state) and SafetyAlert (anomaly type, severity, recommended action). Specify the latency and bandwidth requirements for real-time feedback. Clarify the ownership of data logging and archival (NGHE Solution vs. external data warehouse).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid energy management) directly addresses the refined FR by providing mechanisms for continuous measurement, real-time feedback, control optimization, and data logging. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified; it allows flexibility in implementation (e.g., centralized vs. distributed computation) while maintaining clear functional intent.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystems do not expose energy data via standard interfaces, custom adapters may be needed, increasing Vehicle module complexity. Mitigation: Define energy data interface standards early in Vehicle design.

Risk: If Telemetry bandwidth is insufficient, real-time feedback may be delayed, reducing operator responsiveness. Mitigation: Prioritize energy_per_ton KPI in Telemetry; defer detailed diagnostics to asynchronous channels.

Risk: If SafetyAlert is overloaded with non-safety anomalies, operators may ignore critical alerts. Mitigation: Reserve SafetyAlert for high-severity anomalies (e.g., energy_per_ton > E_target + 20%); use Telemetry for routine feedback.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution provides excellent containment for centralized energy management logic. Vehicle module owns energy sensors and local optimization; NGHE Solution owns global KPI computation and feedback orchestration. Interface boundaries are clear and reusable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: measure energy_per_ton over an evaluation period and compare against E_target. Requires instrumentation of Vehicle (energy sensors) and NGHE Solution (KPI computation). Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces must be tested for latency and reliability.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns KPI computation and feedback; Vehicle owns energy measurement; operators own control decisions. Responsibilities are distinct and testable.

recommended_revisions: Define the data schema for Telemetry (energy consumption, work output, timestamps, vehicle state) and SafetyAlert (anomaly type, severity, recommended action).
Specify latency and bandwidth requirements for real-time feedback (e.g., energy_per_ton update every 10 seconds, SafetyAlert within 5 seconds).
Clarify the boundary between vehicle-level optimization (e.g., gear selection, engine RPM tuning) and centralized KPI feedback.
Define E_target validation criteria (e.g., achievable with selected vehicle, realistic for operational profile).
Specify data logging and archival requirements (e.g., retention period, access controls, post-evaluation analysis tools).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it comprises four sub-FRs (measurement, feedback, control optimization, data logging) that require further decomposition. Each sub-FR has distinct DPs and IMs. Proceeding to the next level will clarify ownership, interfaces, and verification criteria for each sub-function.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved) with sufficient granularity and accuracy to compute energy_per_ton.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time feedback to operators and tele-operators on current energy efficiency status and deviations from target.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall support control strategies (manual, semi-autonomous, or autonomous) that enable operators to optimize energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and archive energy and work data for post-evaluation analysis and continuous improvement.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems expose energy consumption data via standardized interfaces (e.g., CAN bus, OBD-II equivalent).
Network Link (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) provides sufficient bandwidth and latency for real-time feedback.
Operators and tele-operators can interpret energy efficiency feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
E_target is well-defined and achievable with the selected vehicle and operational profile.
Safety criticality (high) is managed through SafetyAlert thresholds and Safety Services integration.
open_risks: Legacy or proprietary vehicle architectures may require custom adapters for energy data exposure.
Network bandwidth constraints or outages may delay real-time feedback.
Operator training and feedback presentation may limit effectiveness of energy optimization.
Unrealistic E_target may lead to unsafe or unproductive control strategies.
SafetyAlert overload may reduce operator responsiveness to critical anomalies.
evidence_needed: Vehicle energy data interface specification (e.g., CAN bus signals, data types, update rates).
Telemetry and SafetyAlert bandwidth and latency requirements.
E_target validation study (e.g., achievability analysis, operational profile impact).
Operator feedback presentation design (e.g., dashboard mockups, usability testing).
Data logging and archival requirements (e.g., retention period, access controls).
Safety criticality assessment for energy efficiency anomalies (e.g., SafetyAlert thresholds).
